title: Optically programmable and erasable cryogenic flash memory on an undoped Si/SiGe heterostructure
authors: S Rastogi, S Samanta, V Jangir, L Patra, K Modi, A Jain, G Scappucci, U Mukhopadhyay, S Mahapatra
abstract: Scalable cryogenic memory is a critical yet unresolved requirement for large-scale quantum computing architectures, particularly for computing-in-memory schemes. We exploit the interplay between optical excitation and gate bias in an undoped Si/SiGe heterojunction field-effect transistor (HFET) to realize non-volatile memory functionality. The device exploits a high interface trap density ($D_{it} > 1.6 \times 10^{12}$~eV$^{-1}$cm$^{-2}$), which, in conjunction with the oxide thickness and dielectric constant, enables effective "locking" of the threshold voltage to the applied gate bias over a wide voltage range. Two of these states can be selected for binary operation, while the availability of multiple stable states within the same device enables multibit data storage. Robust cycling endurance ($>~10^3$ cycles) and long-term state retention ($>~10^4$~s) of the memory states at 1.5 K confirm the suitability of this approach for integration into Si/SiGe-based quantum computing architectures.
summary: This paper reports on the development of an optically programmable and erasable cryogenic flash memory using an undoped Si/SiGe HFET. By leveraging interface traps, the device achieves stable, non-volatile storage of multiple bits at 1.5 K. The results confirm its viability for integration into large-scale, solid-state quantum computing architectures.
main_problem: The paper addresses the critical need for scalable, non-volatile memory elements within cryogenic quantum computing architectures to overcome information shuttling bottlenecks.
main_result: They successfully demonstrated both binary and multi-bit memory operation in an HFET, showing stable state retention for years and endurance exceeding $10^3$ cycles at 1.5 K.
method: The memory functionality is realized by exploiting the interplay between optical excitation (using an LED) and gate bias in an undoped Si/SiGe HFET to deterministically tune and 'lock' the threshold voltage.
model_or_system: The physical platform is an undoped Si/SiGe heterojunction field-effect transistor (HFET) fabricated on a Si (100) substrate. The memory state is stored by trapping charges at the semiconductor-oxide interface, which shifts the threshold voltage ($V_{Th}$).
key_observables: Threshold voltage ($V_{Th}$), state retention time, cycling endurance, and the distribution of $V_{Th}$ across multiple programmed states.
important_parameters: Operation temperature of 1.5 K; high interface trap density ($D_{it} > 1.6 	imes 10^{12} 	ext{ eV}^{-1}	ext{cm}^{-2}$); endurance $> 10^3$ cycles; retention $> 10^4$ s.
main_assumptions: The initial $V_{Th}$ without illumination is unstable, requiring a reset step; state stability relies on charge trapping/detrapping processes at the interface.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic setup, transfer characteristics showing $V_{Th}$ tuning via LED illumination, and quantitative data on state retention over time and endurance over repeated switching cycles for both binary and multi-bit operation.
paper_structure: The paper details the physical mechanism, demonstrates the deterministic tuning of $V_{Th}$ using optical excitation, and then rigorously characterizes the device's reliability by measuring state retention and cycling endurance for both two-state and multi-bit operation.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses hardware requirements for quantum computation by proposing a solid-state, non-volatile memory element compatible with silicon-based quantum platforms, which is highly relevant to quantum information engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
